# W22 Probe AI + AI-2 — AMFC boundary bias

*Auto-generated by `experiments/analyze_probe_aiboundary.py`.*

## Question

Does AMFC degenerate to boundary-only picks on large Pareto fronts? Does data-derived z_ref (NC30 b) cure the bias?

## Probe AI — fixed z_ref (R1=0, R2=0.05)

## AMFC pick concentration vs Pareto front size

n_runs per size: 300

| |P_t| | most-common winner | boundary % | middle % |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | pos 1 | 100.0% | 0.0% |
| 3 | pos 2 | 100.0% | 0.0% |
| 5 | pos 4 | 100.0% | 0.0% |
| 7 | pos 6 | 100.0% | 0.0% |
| 10 | pos 9 | 100.0% | 0.0% |
| 20 | pos 19 | 99.7% | 0.3% |
| 30 | pos 29 | 96.3% | 3.7% |
| 50 | pos 49 | 92.3% | 7.7% |

**Verdict:** at |P_t|=50, 92% of picks are at boundaries → AMFC has STRONG boundary bias on large fronts.

## Probe AI-2 — fixed vs data-derived z_ref

## NC30 b data-derived z_ref vs fixed: boundary-bias interaction

n_runs per size: 300

| |P_t| | fixed-zref boundary % | derived-zref boundary % | argmax disagreement |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 100.0% | 100.0% | 0.0% |
| 5 | 100.0% | 100.0% | 0.0% |
| 10 | 100.0% | 100.0% | 0.0% |
| 20 | 99.3% | 99.3% | 0.3% |
| 30 | 95.7% | 96.0% | 0.3% |

**Verdict:** NC30 b has ASYMMETRIC effect on boundary bias.

- LEFT boundary (pos 0): fixed 0% → derived 0%
  (NC30 b sets R1 = min ROI = leftmost ROI → contribution = 0 → left always loses)
- RIGHT boundary (pos -1): fixed 99% → derived 99%
  (NC30 b sets R2 = max risk = leftmost risk → (R2 - rightmost_risk) = full risk range → right still wins)
- Total boundary %: fixed=99% → derived=99%
- argmax disagreement: 0%

**Implication:** NC30 b structurally NULLS the LEFT (low-ROI) boundary's
contribution but does NOT touch the RIGHT (high-ROI) boundary's advantage.
AMFC under NC30 b will still concentrate on the high-ROI extreme. To kill
the right-boundary bias too, R2 would need to be tighter (e.g., median risk),
but that's geometrically inconsistent with R2 = 'risk we don't want to exceed'.

## Combined verdict (Decision Point #3)

🔴 **AMFC structurally degenerates to right-boundary picks under both z_ref modes.**

Root cause: the rightmost solution's HV contribution `(ROI_{n-1} − ROI_{n-2}) × (R2 − risk_{n-1})`
contains the WHOLE risk range above the front (R2 minus max risk), which is HUGE compared
to any middle solution's neighbor-gap × neighbor-gap. The probe AD rectangle bug does NOT
change this — even Eq 6.36 rectangle preserves `(R2 − risk_{n-1})` at the right edge.

**This is consistent with the production breakthrough's mechanism:**
- AMFC picks essentially the high-ROI extreme of each front consistently
- The +7.50% Δ comes from PER-PORTFOLIO DIFFERENTIATION (NC8c-v2 position carry across
  periods makes each portfolio track its own ROI/risk trajectory), NOT from sophisticated
  AMFC argmax behavior
- The "anticipation arm" amplifies tracking quality of the eventually-selected portfolio,
  not the discriminative power of AMFC's argmax

**Paper implication:** Section §6.4 should acknowledge that AMFC argmax is largely a
right-boundary picker on realistic fronts. The mechanism's value comes from temporal
tracking (KF + position carry), not from clever Pareto-front geometry.

**Action items:**
- Do NOT pursue NC30 b ratification — it doesn't fix the asymmetry that matters
- Consider z_ref normalization (R2 = max risk + small margin) for future experimentation;
  but this changes the comparability of HV proxy across periods → not a paper-grade fix
- The boundary-bias finding is a feature characterization, not a bug to fix
